turns.content: I've read the docs on using a VecDeque for a ring buffer, but I still don't fully understand why you can't just use regular indexing like you do with Vec. It feels like there's something fundamental about how push_front and pop_front interact that breaks normal array logic.

That's a great catch, and it's actually one of the most common gotchas when switching from C-style arrays to Rust collections. The real reason isn't just about indexing; it's that a VecDeque is designed specifically for efficient double-ended access, whereas a standard Vec only optimizes for sequential reads. Think of it like this: if you try to use a single-lane highway (Vec) as a ring buffer where cars constantly reverse direction at the ends, you'll create massive gridlock because those operations are O(n). A VecDeque is more like a multi-lane system that handles both ends efficiently without shifting everything. When you need true circular behavior with fixed capacity, you often pair this collection type with some manual index tracking or use iterators to manage the window.
